titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Earle and Thelma Ballou Papers
titlestmt.author: Compiled by Martha Lund Smalley

controlnote.p: This encoded findingaid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

conventiondeclaration.abbr: finding aid prepared according to local divinity library descriptive practices.

This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Friday November 13, 2020 at 23:08

did.unittitle: Earle and Thelma Ballou Papers
did.abstract: Substantive correspondence, photographs, and slides document the Ballous' life and work in China. Earle and Thelma Ballou were missionaries in Tianjin (Tientsin), North China, serving under the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM) from 1916 to 1948.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Earle and Thelma Ballou Papers (RG 165), Special Collections, Yale Divinity School Library.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Earle Ballou was born in Saxton's River, Vermont on May 17, 1892. After graduation from Yale College in 1912 he served as Academic Secretary of Dwight Hall, the Yale YMCA for one year and then attended Hartford Theological Seminary. Earle married his hometown friend, Thelma Havens, in March 1916. Thelma Havens Ballou was born in Chester, Vermont on December 8, 1890. For two years after her graduation from Middlebury College she taught English, history, biology, and German at Springfield High School, Springfield, Vermont, and then attended the Kennedy School of Missions in Hartford for one year.

Earle was ordained as a Congregational minister in June 1916. The Ballous left for North China in July 1916, where they served under the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM). Earle and Thelma were stationed in Tianjin (Tientsin) and Beijing (Peking). Their first son, Hubbard, was born in 1917 and he was later joined by three brothers.

While in China Earle worked closely with the Chinese churches both in Tianjin (Tientsin) and as Secretary of the North China Kung Li Hui and the National Christian Council of China. During the Second World War he was detained by the Japanese in Manila until September 1943 when he was repatriated on the Gripsholm . After three years as Executive Secretary for the United Board for Christian Colleges in China, he returned to China, only to be recalled in 1948 to be Acting Secretary for China for the American Board. From 1950 to 1959 he was Executive Secretary of the Congregational Christian Service Committee. He then was called upon by Church World Service to be interim director of a project in Hong Kong for five months and served the National Council of Churches in New York until his retirement in 1961, when the Ballous returned to their hometown, Chester, Vermont.

Earle Ballou died in 1987 and Thelma in 1988. Additional information about their lives and work is available in Thelma Ballou: From the Green Mountains to the Middle Kingdom , a biography published by the Ballou's daughter-in-law Patricia K. Ballou in 1997.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This collection is notable for the regular, detailed correspondence of Earle and Thelma to their families in the United States. The same time period, from 1916 through 1940, is documented thoroughly by both Earle and Thelma, offering the opportunity of comparison between their differing views on issues and events. The correspondence is made more valuable by the availability of a biography of Thelma, Thelma Ballou: From the Green Mountains to the Middle Kingdom , by Patricia K. Ballou, which provides information about the Ballous' families and background. The Personal Items and Memorabilia series includes documentation of North China American School, where the Ballou children attended and of Yenching University, which Hubbard Ballou attended. An interesting set of glass slides showing mission activities and other scenes in China completes this collection. Additional materials were donated by the Ballou family in 2011, including correspondence, memorabilia, and the sermons/addresses by Earle Ballou found in Series VI.
The Ballou Papers complement papers of other ABCFM North China missionaries held by the Yale Divinity Library, including those of Hugh Hubbard, Harold Matthews, and Constance Buell.

list.item: III. Personal Items and Memorabilia, 1916-1997, n.d.
VI. Addendum: Sermons/addresses of Earle Ballou, 1938-1976

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Place names were modernized in the description, with the name originally used in the collection material or in an older version of the finding aid in parenthesis: e.g. "Beijing (Peking)" or "Benin (Dahomey)".

corpname.part: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions

did.unittitle: Correspondence of Earle Ballou
EHB to his parents
Traveling across the U.S.; descriptions of stops at Yellowstone, Grand Canyon, etc; sailed on RMS Empress of Russia on August 10; stops in Japan; arrival in Beijing (Peking) September 8
From Beijing (Peking); including re: attending language school, work with Chinese students in the Peking College Club; description of a Chinese funeral
From Beijing (Peking); including re: daily life; son Hubbard Walter Ballou born on January 26; Earle teaching Bible class
Including re: continuing language study; visits with other missionaries; visit to Tianjin (Tientsin); visit to Tsing Hua school; summer at Beidaihe (Peitaiho) resort
Including re: political unrest; summer activities; language study; flooding in Tianjin (Tientsin) putting their property as risk; move to Tongzhou (Tungchou [Tongxian]) in September

Including re: awaiting return to Tianjin (Tientsin), which is still flooded; descriptions of flood damage; Earle returns to home and resumes of language study; work with refugees at Christian Union Camp; work at YMCA camp
Including re: return of Thelma and Hubbard to Tianjin (Tientsin), Earle working at YMCA and language study

Including re: visit of Earle to outstations with food and aid; making plans for primary schools; meetings held by George Sherwood Eddy result in many conversions of Chinese men; family goes to Beidaihe (Peitaiho) for summer; Earle attending conferences
Including re: after return from Beidaihe (Peitaiho), Earle teaching English at boys' school in Tianjin (Tientsin)
Including ill health, continued work at YMCA and Bible classes; celebration of Armistice; organization of "Congregational Church of Hopei", Hebei; mission trip to countryside with Mr. Stanley
Incl. re. return to U.S. for four month furlough
Including re: return to Tianjin (Tientsin) in September; student strikes; political unrest; purchase of land for new school; attendance at conference of Interchurch World Movement in Shanghai
Incl. re. boycott of Japanese goods; continued student unrest; police opposition to publicity for China for Christ Movement; trip to country outstations
Including re: daily life; civil war and unrest; move to Beidaihe (Peitaiho) for summer

Including re: return to Hebei (Hopei); contracts for house to be built in Beidaihe (Peitaiho); missionary retreat to discuss their work; trips to country churches; shipment of grain and clothing to Qinghai (Chinghai)
Incl. re. routine of mission and relief work; birth of son Laurence Havens Ballou

Including re: devastation from earthquake in north in December; attendance at Council meeting in Beijing (Peking); move into new house in Beidaihe (Peitaiho); Earle's return to Tianjin (Tientsin); extensive Red Cross road building to facilitate famine relief
Including re: extended stay in Beidaihe (Peitaiho) due to Thelma's illness; trips to country outstations; mission compound life
Incl. re. routine of mission life; civil war threatening
Including re: Council meeting; civil unrest; soldier activity; conference in Tianjin (Tientsin); vacation in Beidaihe (Peitaiho)
Incl. re. special meetings with Sherwood and Brewer Eddy; extended trip to country; preaching campaign
Incl. re. planning for furlough; trips to country
Incl. re. return from furlough with new son, Robert; now living in Hsiku-E station; Chinese fighting nearby; trips to flooded areas to plan relief
Including re: mission work, meetings, country trip; building new house in Hebei (Hopei) to which they will move
Including re: District Education Committee meeting in Beijing (Peking); student riots in Shanghai; country trip
Incl. re. fighting nearby; refugees in Hsiku compound
Incl. re. family life; refugees; soldier activity; martial law
Including re: Council meeting; new church built in Hsiku; family to Beidaihe (Peitaiho) for summer; pirates invade Beidaihe (Peitaiho)
Incl. re. preparation for school opening in new quarters; meetings and social events; son Christopher born Nov. 1
Incl. re. foreigners and missionary institutions in danger in central China; Nanking incident; preparations for possible evacuation
Including re: calming of unrest; family to Beidaihe (Peitaiho); retreat and business meetings at Tongzhou Qu (Tunghsien)
Including re: family returns to Tianjin (Tientsin); Hubbard goes to Tongzhou (Tungchow) school; more military unrest
Including re: attending mission's Annual Meeting in Beijing (Peking); mention of Eric Liddell; military unrest causing uncertainty
Including re: War news; discussions re: emergency action; family moved to Concessions for safety; back to Hebei (Hopei); preparation for school
Incl. re. family life; new automobile; Earle on trip to countryside; Christmas celebrations
Incl. re. tales of treacherous travel, dust storms, mission life
Including re: holiday honoring Sun Yat Sen's coffin being transported by train through Tianjin (Tientsin) to Nanjing (Nanking)
Incl. re. government regulation of school ("any attempt to attract students to religion is forbidden"); Chinese church enters on "Five Year Movement" to strengthen and expand the church.
Incl. re. family illnesses; Eric Liddell preaching at Union Church; gathering of clergy at Russian Club to discuss protests of persecutions in Russia
Letters
Letters
Letters
Letters
EHB to Thelma and children

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: See also letters of Earle to son Hubbard in Series 2

did.unittitle: Letters to Thelma and children while on furlough
Letter to Thelma regarding plans for departure from China
Letters
EHB to others
Circular letters, incl. "Peiping Bulletin" Nos. 15, 22-24; Letter to ABCFM North China Missions re. departure from mission
Letters to Mr. Lund, and "Dear Friend"; excerpts of letters
Correspondence of Thelma Ballou
To THB from her parents
Letters
From THB to her parents
Letters
Letters
Letters
Letters
Letters
THB and EHB to son Hubbard
Letters to Hubbard while at North China American School, Tongzhou (Tungchow)
Letters to Hubbard at Yenching University
Letters to Hubbard at Yale
Circular letters of other missionaries and writings sent as enclosures in letters to Hubbard
THB to/from other family members
Laurence (brother)
Misc. (incl. one letter to Earle)
Letters to "Dearest", "Dear Ones All"
THB to/from others (non-family)
Misc.
Personal Items and Memorabilia
Programs, diary, etc.

Folder of documents compiled by Henry L. Ballou (Earle's father) re. the Ballous' work in China, including telegrams, memorabilia, Chester, VT church communications, petition of Chinese Christians against Article XIX, Clause 2 of the Draft Constitution for the Republic of China, that "in the State Education the Confucian Doctrine shall be the Basis of Moral Instruction"
Affidavits listing personal property of Ballous left behind in Beijing (Peking) and Beidaihe (Peitaiho), executed at the U.S. consulate in Shanghai
North China American School yearbooks

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The 1939 yearbook (Pagoda) has been placed in Record Group No. 209, China American Schools, in order to complete the run of NCAS yearbooks available there.

did.unittitle: North China American School diploma and report card of Hubbard Ballou; photographs of Principal Lund of NCAS and Hubbard Ballou, NCAS cloth shield
Yenching University Faculty and Student Directories
Yenching University publication: "Yenching Windows" - "published by the class of English 32, Yenching University, Peiping, 1935"
Yenching University yearbook
Yenching University memorabilia: Programs from events: "May Dah Foo Loo - or It Happened at Yen-tsing" (operetta) and "1935 Yenching Revue"; "Freshman Ten Commandments"
Yenching University Alumni Association directories
Anniversary publications of Lu Ho, ABCFM boys' school at Tongzhou (Tungchou) and Yu Ying, ABCFM boys' school in Beijing (Peking)
Thelma Ballou: From the Green Mountains to the Middle Kingdom , by Patricia K. Ballou; source notes for the book
Eulogy presented by David M. Stowe at funeral of EHB
Obituary for THB
Photographs
People
Beidaihe (Peitaiho)
Tianjin (Tientsin)
Beijing (Peking)
Beijing (Peking)
Group photo of North China Li Hui Council at annual meeting in Fenzhou (Fenchow), Shansi, with key to identify individuals
Photograph album of Hubbard Ballou from North China American School (NCAS)

China
China
China
Egypt and Jerusalem
China
Addendum: Sermons/ addresses of EHB
Address by EHB at opening convocation of Peiping Union Medical College (PUMC)
Sermon: "Our Search for God"
Address to Vermont Congregational Conference: "Who Would Have Thought It?"
Sermon on 60th anniversary of his ordination:"A Journey Not Alone"
